question: Based on Figure 1.2-5 (cutaway) and Figure 5.1-2 (RCS diagram), is the vertical RPV arrangement adequate for establishing natural circulation?

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It lists the relevant diagrams and legends for both I&C and piping and instrumentation diagrams, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0044.title: Chemical and Volume Control System
nuscale_ch01_0044.summary: The Chemical and Volume Control System section describes the NuScale reactor’s passive safety features, including two independent decay heat removal trains, each capable of fully cooling the reactor core using natural circulation and passive condensers. In the event of a steam generator tube failure or loss of AC power, the system isolates the affected components and passively removes heat to a large, stainless steel-lined ultimate heat sink pool, ensuring continued cooling and safety without external power. The spent fuel pool is cooled independently, and the reactor pool has sufficient water inventory to manage heat removal during accidents.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes special plant features of the NuScale Power Plant, including the Central Utility Building (CUB) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGB), which house various utility and backup systems but are not safety-related. It also highlights human factors considerations, such as fail-safe design, centralized multi-module control, digital interfaces, and workload management, all aimed at minimizing human error and meeting regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0019.title: Operating Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0019.summary: The NuScale Power Module (NPM) operates using natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, and features a small, modular, integral pressurized water reactor design. It relies on passive safety systems—including dedicated emergency core cooling and decay heat removal systems—that do not require electrical power, and its components are partially immersed in a reactor pool for enhanced safety and heat removal. The NPM allows for flexible power maneuvering through control rods and boron concentration adjustments, with all major components integrated to minimize external piping and penetrations.

nuscale_ch01_0032.title: Radioactive Waste Management System
nuscale_ch01_0032.summary: The section describes the radioactive waste management system at the NuScale Power Plant, noting that liquid, gaseous, and solid waste systems are detailed in Chapter 11, along with effluent monitoring. It also outlines the general arrangement of major structures, focusing on the Reactor Building (RXB), which is a reinforced, seismically designed concrete structure housing the nuclear power modules and essential systems, and provides radiation protection for personnel.

nuscale_ch01_0033.title: General Arrangement of Major Structures and Equipment
nuscale_ch01_0033.summary: This section describes the general arrangement of major structures and equipment at a NuScale Power Plant, focusing on the central placement and design of the Reactor Building (RXB). The RXB is a reinforced concrete, Seismic Category I structure designed for safety and radiation protection, housing the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) in a common reactor pool with individual bays arranged in two rows. The section also references detailed figures and chapters for further information on radioactive waste management and building layouts.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section describes the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS), which uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to ensure safe and efficient cooling, maintain proper water chemistry, and provide uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping to transfer heat from the reactor core to the steam generator, ensuring safe and efficient operation. The RCS design prioritizes maintaining coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support plant safety during normal and abnormal conditions. Each NuScale Power Module (NPM) contains its own RCS, as described in this section.

nuscale_ch01_0000.title: Preface
nuscale_ch01_0000.summary: The Preface outlines the copyright and usage restrictions for the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application, specifying that only the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) is authorized to use, copy, or disclose the document as needed for regulatory purposes. It also notes that proprietary information is protected, and any copies made by the NRC must include the appropriate copyright and proprietary notices.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the basis for establishing operational pressure and temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) to comply with regulatory requirements over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It describes the methodology used to calculate pressure-temperature limit curves, accounting for vessel embrittlement due to neutron exposure, and provides the relevant figures and tables for these limits to ensure protection against non-ductile failure.

nuscale_ch05_0017.title: Reactor Vessel Integrity
nuscale_ch05_0017.summary: This section outlines the procedures and regulatory compliance measures to ensure the integrity of the NuScale reactor vessel over its 60-year design life. It details the development of operating procedures to maintain pressure-temperature limits, evaluates the vessel’s resistance to pressurized thermal shock and upper-shelf energy degradation, and confirms that predicted material properties remain within regulatory safety criteria throughout the vessel’s operational lifespan.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance by establishing steady-state parameters—such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures—across different power levels using thermal hydraulic modeling and verification. It details how uncertainties and design considerations are addressed to ensure efficient operation, maximize electrical generation, and maintain safety margins, particularly emphasizing the low pressure losses that support the plant’s natural circulation design.

nuscale_ch01_0743.title: SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5, Rev 2: Startup of an Inactive Loop or Recirculation Loop at an Incorrect Temperature, and Flow Controller Malfunction Causing an Increase in BWR Core Flow RateII.C Events of Moderate Frequency
nuscale_ch01_0743.summary: This section explains that the regulatory guidance in SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5, which addresses startup of inactive or recirculation loops at incorrect temperatures and flow controller malfunctions in PWR and BWR designs, is not applicable to the NuScale reactor. This is because the NuScale design does not use reactor coolant pumps or forced coolant flow, and relevant accident scenarios are instead addressed under DSRS 15.4.6.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for degasification and emergency core cooling to maintain safety and integrity. The system interfaces with several other subsystems, ensuring effective operation and containment of radionuclides.

nuscale_ch01_0058.title: Steam Generator Thermal-Hydraulic Performance Testing - Electrically Heated Facility
nuscale_ch01_0058.summary: This section describes the critical heat flux (CHF) testing conducted for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. The tests aimed to gather CHF data, validate and develop CHF correlations for NuScale applications, and collect thermal-hydraulic performance data under various conditions using an electrically heated 5x5 simulated fuel bundle at the AREVA Karlstein facility.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and supports key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various system components and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to help control system pressure. Each component’s functions and connections are briefly outlined, with references to further detailed sections.

nuscale_ch01_0811.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.5 Detect and Suppress Method Trip of reactor before SAFDL violation
nuscale_ch01_0811.summary: The NuScale design does not use the exclusion zone or detect-and-suppress (D&S) methods for thermal hydraulic stability; instead, existing reactor trip signals act preemptively to prevent violation of safety limits before flow instabilities can develop. The system partially conforms to regulatory criteria by ensuring reactor trips occur before SAFDL violations, but does not actively detect or suppress instabilities, and backup D&S options are not required due to the design’s inherent protections.

nuscale_ch01_0057.title: Critical Heat Flux Testing - NuFuel HTP2™ Fuel Design
nuscale_ch01_0057.summary: This section describes the critical heat flux (CHF) testing conducted for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. The testing aimed to gather CHF data to develop and validate NuScale's CHF correlations, as well as to measure bundle subchannel exit temperatures and pressure drop characteristics under various hydraulic conditions. The tests were performed using a 5x5 electrically-heated simulated fuel bundle at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics facility in Germany.

edges.description: Section A provides the detailed diagrams (including RPV arrangement) that visually clarify the system layout referenced in Section B's description of the natural circulation cooling system, enabling assessment of whether the vertical RPV arrangement supports natural circulation.

Section A provides detailed diagrams, including the vertical RPV arrangement, which visually clarify the design referenced in Section B's description of natural circulation as the primary coolant flow mechanism, thus supporting the assessment of adequacy.

Section A provides diagrams and drawings related to the plant's piping and instrumentation, which can be used to understand the arrangement of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and associated systems, while Section B describes the general plant layout and references figures that show the arrangement of major structures and equipment, thus both sections provide visual and descriptive information that together clarify the physical configuration relevant to assessing natural circulation in the RPV.

Section A provides detailed piping and instrumentation diagrams that visually clarify the design and arrangement of components, while Section B describes the general arrangement of major structures and equipment, with both sections offering complementary information necessary to assess whether the vertical RPV arrangement supports natural circulation.

Section B states that the NPM is designed to use natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, while Section A describes the system features (such as decay heat removal and passive condenser placement) that support and enable this natural circulation, thus providing evidence that the vertical RPV arrangement is adequate.

Section B provides the physical layout of major structures and equipment, which includes the vertical RPV arrangement referenced in the analysis question, while Section A describes the functional design of the natural circulation cooling system that operates within that arrangement.

Section A describes the operational features and safety systems that rely on natural circulation, while Section B outlines the design basis of the RCS, explicitly stating that the system depends on natural circulation without pumps, thus Section A supports the adequacy of the vertical RPV arrangement described in Section B for establishing natural circulation.

Section A describes the operating characteristic that the NPM uses natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, while Section B references the general arrangement of major structures and equipment (including the RPV's vertical orientation as depicted in referenced figures), thus Section B provides the physical context that supports the feasibility described in Section A.

Section A describes the operating characteristic that the NPM uses natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, while Section B specifies that the RCS design relies on natural circulation without pumps, thus Section A's operational intent is satisfied by Section B's design basis.

Section A provides the physical arrangement of major structures and equipment, which is referenced by Section B's design basis description of the RCS relying on natural circulation, connecting the adequacy of the vertical RPV arrangement to the system's ability to establish natural circulation.

Section A describes the reliance on natural circulation in the RCS design, while Section B provides diagrams (including Figure 5.1-2) that visually clarify the arrangement of components such as the vertical RPV, connecting the textual claim to its physical implementation.

Section A describes the RCS's reliance on natural circulation enabled by the vertical RPV arrangement, while Section B details supporting systems (decay heat removal) that depend on and reinforce the effectiveness of this natural circulation design.

Section A provides the general context and introduction to the plant design, while Section B specifies that detailed diagrams (such as the RCS diagram) are provided to visually clarify design features relevant to questions like natural circulation adequacy.

Section B describes the closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system enabled by the vertical RPV arrangement depicted in the figures referenced in the analysis question, thereby providing evidence that supports the adequacy of the design for natural circulation.

Section B describes the closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system enabled by the vertical RPV arrangement depicted in Figure 1.2-5, while Section A focuses on operating procedures to maintain vessel integrity, thus Section B provides system-level context that supports the adequacy of the vertical RPV arrangement for natural circulation as questioned in the analysis.

Section A describes the overall RCS design and its reliance on natural circulation, while Section B details specific ASME code compliance for RPV attachments, which supports the structural integrity necessary for the RPV's vertical arrangement to function as intended for natural circulation.

Section A describes the RCS's reliance on natural circulation enabled by the vertical RPV arrangement, while Section B addresses operating procedures to ensure safe pressure-temperature limits during operation, thus Section A's design features provide the physical basis that Section B's procedures must accommodate.

Section A provides technical details and code compliance for RPV attachments, while Section B discusses the development of operating procedures to ensure pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded, both of which are connected through their focus on maintaining reactor vessel integrity under operational conditions.

Section A discusses the system evaluation criteria for the NuScale Power Plant, referencing the arrangement and function of components like the steam generators (SGs), while Section B provides the detailed diagrams (including the RCS diagram) that visually clarify the physical arrangement and connections of these components, thus supporting the evaluation in Section A.

Section A discusses the system evaluation criteria for optimal performance of the NuScale plant, including the use of steam generators (SGs) for decay heat removal, while Section B describes the closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system and the configuration of decay heat removal equipment connected to each SG loop, thus both sections provide complementary technical details that together inform the adequacy of the vertical RPV arrangement for natural circulation.

Section A explains that the NuScale design does not use reactor coolant pumps and thus relies on natural circulation, while Section B provides diagrams (such as the RCS diagram) that visually clarify the system's arrangement, supporting the assessment of whether the vertical RPV arrangement enables natural circulation.

Section A explains that the NuScale design does not use reactor coolant pumps and relies on natural circulation, while Section B describes the system's closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling arrangement, thus Section B provides the technical basis that supports Section A's claim regarding the adequacy of natural circulation in the vertical RPV arrangement.

Section A describes the functional aspects of the RCS and its components relevant to natural circulation, while Section B provides the detailed diagrams (including Figure 5.1-2) that visually represent these components and their arrangement, allowing verification of the described system's adequacy for natural circulation.

Section A describes the vertical RPV arrangement and its features for gas removal and pressure boundary integrity, while Section B explains that the RCS operates as a closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation system, indicating that the vertical arrangement in A supports the natural circulation function described in B.

Section A describes the system evaluation and design considerations for natural circulation in the NuScale reactor, while Section B clarifies that certain regulatory criteria related to forced flow and coolant pumps are not applicable because the NuScale design relies on natural circulation, directly connecting the design approach in A to the regulatory context in B.

Section A discusses the evaluation of system performance parameters, including the use of the steam generator (SG) for decay heat removal, while Section B describes the RCS features such as degasification and pressure boundary integrity, both of which are relevant to assessing whether the vertical RPV arrangement supports natural circulation as depicted in the referenced figures.

Section A describes operational procedures and system component interactions relevant to establishing natural circulation, while Section B provides the referenced diagrams (such as Figure 1.2-5 and Figure 5.1-2) that visually clarify the arrangement and flow paths discussed in Section A.

Section A describes the operational steps involving the chemical volume control system and pressurizer water level adjustments necessary to prepare the RPV for natural circulation, while Section B outlines the design and function of the decay heat removal system, including its reliance on natural circulation through the RCS and SGs; thus, Section A's procedures are prerequisites for enabling the natural circulation capability described in Section B.

Section A outlines the regulatory requirement for thermal hydraulic stability and the need for reactor trip before SAFDL violation, while Section B provides the detailed RCS diagrams (including the vertical RPV arrangement) that visually clarify the design features relevant to assessing natural circulation adequacy.

Section B describes the closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system and its components, which provides the design basis for evaluating whether the vertical RPV arrangement (referenced in the analysis question) can establish natural circulation, while Section A outlines the regulatory requirement to detect and suppress thermal hydraulic instabilities before safety limits are violated; thus, Section B's system description provides evidence relevant to satisfying Section A's regulatory criteria.

Section B describes the reactor's closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation cooling system, which relies on the vertical RPV arrangement depicted in Figure 1.2-5 and Figure 5.1-2, while Section A provides critical heat flux data for the fuel design that supports the safe operation of this natural circulation system.

Section A provides critical heat flux performance data for the NuScale fuel design, which supports the evaluation of natural circulation capability in the vertical RPV arrangement depicted in Section B's system component description and diagram.

Section A provides critical heat flux (CHF) test data and correlations for the NuScale fuel design, which are used to validate that the reactor's thermal-hydraulic performance (including natural circulation in the vertical RPV arrangement) meets the regulatory requirements in Section B for preventing SAFDL violations via reactor trip before unstable flow develops.

Section A describes the system components and operational steps (including RPV arrangement) relevant to establishing natural circulation, while Section B outlines the regulatory requirement to prevent thermal hydraulic instability and ensure protective actions (such as reactor trip) occur before safety limits are violated; thus, the adequacy of the vertical RPV arrangement in Section A is evaluated against the stability and protection criteria in Section B.

Section A describes system operational steps and component arrangements relevant to establishing natural circulation, while Section B provides critical heat flux test data and correlations for the fuel design, which are necessary to validate that the vertical RPV arrangement (from Section A) can safely support natural circulation without exceeding thermal limits.

Section B provides critical heat flux (CHF) test data and correlations for the NuScale fuel design, which Section A references as the basis for ensuring that reactor trip signals prevent CHF limit violations, thereby supporting the adequacy of the vertical RPV arrangement for natural circulation.
